text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. R. Patel
Riverside Health Centre

12 Mill Lane, Southwark
London SE1 2AB
Dear Dr A. R. Patel
Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H928374 NHS No. 8037164925
Flat 3, 29 Willow Court, Peckham, London SE15 4JT 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, drug-resistant; history of mixed seizure types with nocturnal predominance; cumulative fatigue with perceived worsening in the context of irregular shift work in a care setting with frequent patient transfers.

Imaging/EEG: MRI 2017 reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Prior routine EEGs with intermittent left temporal sharp transients; no definitive lateralising lesion captured on brief recordings.

Previous medication: Lamotrigine (ongoing), Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), Carbamazepine (dizziness, hyponatraemia), Sodium Valproate (weight gain, tremor), Clobazam (sedation), Lacosamide (no clear benefit), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty), Zonisamide (renal colic), Perampanel (mood change), Pregabalin (somnolence), Rufinamide (GI upset). Vagus nerve stimulation discussed in past but not pursued.

Current medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; Midazolam 10 mg buccal PRN for prolonged convulsive seizures; Vitamin D supplementation.

I reviewed Daniel Harris in clinic today, accompanied by his father. He works in a residential care home where breaks are irregular and he is often involved in frequent patient transfers. He reports accumulating fatigue across the week with poorer sleep consolidation. He and his parents have noticed increased recovery time after seizures, more bruising from sudden drops, and reduced confidence mobilising unaccompanied. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. However, functionally he is deteriorating: he is missing more work days, requires more supervision at home, and has described worsening morning confusion and headaches consistent with sleep fragmentation. He denies new infectious triggers or recent head injury. No alcohol or recreational drugs. He uses a smartphone seizure diary which corroborates clustered drops particularly after late shifts.

Examination today: Alert and orientated. Speech fluent. No focal neurological deficit. No tongue laceration currently; healing ecchymosis over right forearm consistent with recent drop. Blood pressure 122/74 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular. BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

Risk/safety: He reports two nocturnal convulsions this past week without supervision as his parents were away one night; he used his bed sensor alarm on the second occasion. No status epilepticus. No recent aspiration, but he describes biting inside cheek. No known pregnancy considerations. Driving: he is not driving and understands ongoing DVLA restrictions.

Work and function: Care setting duties with manual handling appear to aggravate fatigue and perceived seizure burden. We discussed reasonable adjustments at work (regular protected breaks, reduced overnight duties, avoiding lone working for transfers) and he will raise this with occupational health.

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for now; adherence good. We discussed options including revisiting VNS and consideration of inpatient video-EEG telemetry to clarify seizure semiology and assess candidacy for neuromodulation. He is open to VNS; I have provided written information and will refer to the device clinic.
- Arrange 5–7 day home sleep study and actigraphy via community sleep service to quantify sleep fragmentation, given nocturnal predominance and daytime fatigue.
- Bloods: U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D, FBC, lamotrigine trough level, and HbA1c (rule out metabolic contributors to fatigue).
- Safety: reinforce use of bed rails as appropriate, padded headboard, avoid baths unsupervised, ensure buccal midazolam is in-date and that family trained; employer to review manual handling plan.
- Occupational health letter to support phased adjustments including structured breaks and minimising consecutive late shifts.
- Seizure diary: continue electronic log with tags for shift pattern, sleep duration, perceived stress, and meal times to correlate with clusters.
- Follow-up: telephone review by epilepsy nurse specialist in 4 weeks to reassess clusters and injuries; clinic review with me in 3 months or earlier if further deterioration (injuries, prolonged convulsions, status, or intolerable side-effects).

I emphasise that while the absolute seizure pattern appears long-standing, his functional trajectory is deteriorating with cumulative fatigue, more injuries from drop attacks, and increasing post-ictal recovery time in the context of irregular work patterns. We will proceed with the above investigations and VNS pathway discussion to mitigate further decline.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week". It also mentions "drop attacks occurring in batches" and "tonic seizures during both day and night" but without numeric frequencies. Per the rule to select the highest frequency, we use the explicit numeric rate provided. Convert '2 - 3' to '2 to 3' and express as per week.
seizure_frequency_number: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week
reference: Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change
